6.3.10

Plus operation
Plus operation (input1 + input2) → return: output
Where: output is a dbValue, input1 and input2 are dbValues defined by expressions.
This function returns the value of input1.value plus input2.value.
output.value = input1.value + input2.value
If both inputs are not constant values, the returned quality flag is input1.qFlag | input2.qFlag, and
the output timestamp is equal to the most recent of both inputs.
If only one of the inputs is not a constant value, the returned quality flag and timestamp are equal
to this input values for quality and timestamp.
If both inputs are constants, the returned quality and timestamp are equal to the second input
values for quality and timestamp.
This function is implemented according to the following pseudo-code:

output.value = input1.value + input2.value
If (input1.type != CONST)
→ output.qFlag = input1.qFlag && output.tSpec = input1.tSpec
If(input2.type != CONST) && input2.timestamp > input1.timestamp)
→ output.qFlag |= input2.qFlag
→ if(input2.tSpec > input1.tSpec)
→ output.tSpec = input2.tSpec
Else
→ output.qFlag = input2.qFlag && output.tSpec = input2.tSpec

If any of the input values are equal to NAN, the result value will be set as NAN (0 at status or
command) with invalid output quality flag
Example
input1 + input2
Given that input1 is a coreDb point name, this function will return the result of:
output.value = input1.value + 3
output.qFlag = input1.qFlag
output.tSpec = input1.tSpec

Pag 148

User Manual – Easergy Builder

06/2024

6.3.11

Minus Operation
Minus Operation (input1 - input2) → return: output
Where: output is a dbValue, input1 and input2 are dbValues defined by expressions.
This function returns the value of input1.value minus input2.value.
output.value = input1.value - input2.value
If both inputs are not constant values, the returned quality flag is input1.qFlag | input2.qFlag, and
the output timestamp is equal to the most recent of both inputs.
If only one of the inputs is not a constant value, the returned quality flag and timestamp are equal
to this input values for quality and timestamp.
If both inputs are constants, the returned quality and timestamp are equal to the second input
values for quality and timestamp.
This function is implemented according to the following pseudo-code:

output.value = input1.value - input2.value
If(input1.type != CONST)
→ output.qFlag = input1.qFlag && output.tSpec = input1.tSpec
If(input2.type != CONST) && input2.timestamp > input1.timestamp)
→ output.qFlag |= input2.qFlag
→ if(input2.tSpec > input1.tSpec)
→ output.tSpec = input2.tSpec
Else
→ output.qFlag = input2.qFlag && output.tSpec = input2.tSpec

If any of the input values are equal to NAN, the result value will be set as NAN (0 at status or
command) with invalid output quality flag.
Example
-(-input1 – input2 – MIN(4, input3))
Given that input1, input2 and input3 are coreDb point names, the function will return, once each
parameter gets its values:
output.value = - (((input1.value - input2.value) - MIN(4,input3))). Calculating it from left to right.
output.qFlag = input1.qFlag | input2.qFlag | MIN(4, input3).qFlag. In this case, the output quality
flag is adding the quality flag values from each input, with type different to constant. The last one, is
the returned value of MIN(4, input3) quality flag returned.
output.tSpec will be equal to the most recent between input1.tSpec, input2.tSpec and the returned
timestamp from MIN(4, input3) calculation.

Pag 149

User Manual – Easergy Builder

06/2024

6.3.12

Multiplication Operation
Multiplication Operation (input1*input2) → return: output
Where: output is a dbValue, input1 and input2 are dbValues defined by expressions.
This function returns the value of input1.value plus input2.value.
output.value = input1.value * input2.value
If both inputs are not constant values, the returned quality flag is input1.qFlag | input2.qFlag, and
the output timestamp is equal to the most recent of both inputs.
If only one of the inputs is not a constant value, the returned quality flag and timestamp are equal
to this input values for quality and timestamp.
If both inputs are constants, the returned quality and timestamp are equal to the second input
values for quality and timestamp.
This function is implemented according to the following pseudo-code:

output.value = input1.value * input2.value
If (input1.type != CONST)
→ output.qFlag = input1.qFlag && output.tSpec = input1.tSpec
If(input2.type != CONST) && input2.timestamp > input1.timestamp)
→ output.qFlag |= input2.qFlag
→ if(input2.tSpec > input1.tSpec)
→ output.tSpec = input2.tSpec
Else
→ output.qFlag = input2.qFlag && output.tSpec = input2.tSpec

If any of the input values are equal to NAN, the result value will be set as NAN (0 at status or
command) with invalid output quality flag.
Example
(input1 + input2) * 4 + MIN(4,input3)
Given that input1, input2 and input3 are coreDb point names, the function will return, once each
parameter gets its values:
Firstly, it calculates the value of input1.value+input2.value, then this result is multiplicated by 4 and,
finally, the value of calculate MIN(4,input3) is added:
output.value = (((input1.value + input2.value)*4) + MIN(4,input3))
Calculating it from left to right, having into account that multiplication has higher precedence than
plus operation, but less than brackets.
output.qFlag = input1.qFlag | input2.qFlag | MIN(4, input3).qFlag
In this case, the output quality flag is adding the quality flag values from each input, with type
different to constant. The last one, is the returned value of MIN(4, input3) quality flag returned.
output.tSpec will be equal to the most recent between input1.tSpec, input2.tSpec and the returned
timestamp from MIN(4, input3) calculation.

Pag 150

User Manual – Easergy Builder

06/2024

6.3.13

Division Operation
Division Operation (input1/input2) → return: output
Where: output is a dbValue, input1 and input2 are dbValue defined by expressions.
This function returns the value of input1.value divided by input2.value.
output.value = input1.value / input2.value
If both inputs are not constant values, the returned quality flag is input1.qFlag | input2.qFlag, and
the output timestamp is equal to the most recent of both inputs.
If only one of the inputs is not a constant value, the returned quality flag and timestamp are equal
to this input values for quality and timestamp.
If both inputs are constants, the returned quality and timestamp are equal to the second input
values for quality and timestamp.
This function is implemented according to the following pseudo-code:

output.value = input1.value / input2.value
If (input1.type != CONST)
→ output.qFlag = input1.qFlag && output.tSpec = input1.tSpec
If(input2.type != CONST) && input2.timestamp > input1.timestamp)
→ output.qFlag |= input2.qFlag
→ if(input2.tSpec > input1.tSpec)
→ output.tSpec = input2.tSpec
Else
→ output.qFlag = input2.qFlag && output.tSpec = input2.tSpec

If any of the input values are equal to NAN, the result value will be set as NAN (0 at status or
command) with invalid output quality flag. Besides, if the second input value is equal to 0, the
returned value will be also NAN (0 at status or command) with invalid output quality flag.
Example
3 + input1 / NOT(input2)
Given that input1 and input2 coreDb point names, the function will return, once each parameter
gets its values:
Firstly, it calculates the value of input1.value/NOT(input2), then 3 is added to this previous result.
output.value = (input1.value / NOT(input2)) + 3
Calculating it from left to right. Besides, division calculation has higher precedence than plus or
minus operation.
output.qFlag = input1.qFlag | NOT( input2).qFlag.
In this case, the output quality flag is adding the quality flag values from each input, with type
different to constant.
The last one, is the returned value of NOT(input2) quality flag returned. output.tSpec will be equal
to the most recent between input1.tSpec and the returned timestamp from NOT(input2) calculation.
If NOT(input2) result value is equal to 0 (so input2.value is different to 0), when the division
operation is done, its result is equal to NaN (0 at status or command) and it is inherited to the
complete formula.

Pag 151

User Manual – Easergy Builder

06/2024

Pag 152

User Manual – Easergy Builder

06/2024

6.3.14

Greater Conditional Operation
Greater Conditional Operation (input1>input2) → return:
output
Where: output is a dbValue, input1 and input2 are dbValue defined by expressions.
This function returns 1 if the value of input1 is greater than input2.value. Otherwise, the returned
value is 0.
If both inputs are not constant values, the returned quality flag is input1.qFlag | input2.qFlag, and
the output timestamp is equal to the most recent of both inputs.
If only one of the inputs is not a constant value, the returned quality flag and timestamp are equal
to this input values for quality and timestamp.
If both inputs are constants, the returned quality and timestamp are equal to the second input
values for quality and timestamp.
Conditional operators have the lowest precedence between the operators.
This function is implemented according to the following pseudo-code:

If(input1.value > input2.value)
→ output.value = 1;
Else
→ output.value = 0;
If (input1.type != CONST)
→ output.qFlag = input1.qFlag && output.tSpec = input1.tSpec
If(input2.type != CONST) && input2.timestamp > input1.timestamp)
→ output.qFlag |= input2.qFlag
→ if(input2.tSpec > input1.tSpec)
→ output.tSpec = input2.tSpec
Else
→

output.qFlag = input2.qFlag && output.tSpec = input2.tSpec

If any of the input values are equal to NAN, the result value will be set as NAN (0 at status or
command) with invalid output quality flag.
Example
input1 > 2
Given that input1 is coreDb point name, this function will return 1 if input1.value is greater than 2. If
not, the returned value is equal to 0:
output.qFlag = input1.qFlag
output.tSpec = input1.tSpec

Pag 153

User Manual – Easergy Builder

06/2024

6.3.15

Less Conditional Operation
Less Conditional Operation (input1<input2) → return:
output
Where: output is a dbValue, input1 and input2 are dbValue defined by expressions.
This function returns 1 if the value of input1 is LESS than input2.value. Otherwise, the returned
value is 0.
If both inputs are not constant values, the returned quality flag is input1.qFlag | input2.qFlag, and
the output timestamp is equal to the most recent of both inputs.
If only one of the inputs is not a constant value, the returned quality flag and timestamp are equal
to this input values for quality and timestamp.
If both inputs are constants, the returned quality and timestamp are equal to the second input
values for quality and timestamp.
Conditional operators have the lowest precedence between the operators.
This function is implemented according to the following pseudo-code:

If(input1.value < input2.value)
→ output.value = 1;
Else
→ output.value = 0;
If (input1.type != CONST)
→ output.qFlag = input1.qFlag && output.tSpec = input1.tSpec
If(input2.type != CONST) && input2.timestamp > input1.timestamp)
→ output.qFlag |= input2.qFlag
→ if(input2.tSpec > input1.tSpec)
→ output.tSpec = input2.tSpec
Else
→ output.qFlag = input2.qFlag && output.tSpec = input2.tSpec

If any of the input values are equal to NAN, the result value will be set as NAN (0 at status or
command) with invalid output quality flag.
Example
(input1 + input2) * 4 < MIN(4,input3)
Given that input1, input2 and input3 are coreDb point names, the function will return, once each
parameter gets its values:
Firstly, on one side, it calculates the value of input1.value+input2.value, and then this result is
multiplicated by 4. On another side, MIN(4,input3) is calculated. Both sides are compared, and if
the first one is the lowest, the output.value is equal to 1. If not, output.value is equal to 0.
output.qFlag = input1.qFlag | input2.qFlag | MIN(4, input3).qFlag. In this case, the output quality
flag is adding the quality flag values from each input, with type different to constant. The last one, is
the returned value of MIN(4, input3) quality flag returned.
output.tSpec will be equal to the most recent between input1.tSpec, input2.tSpec and the returned
timestamp from MIN(4, input3) calculation

Pag 154

User Manual – Easergy Builder

06/2024

6.3.16

Equal Conditional Operation
Equal Conditional Operation (input1==input2) → return:
output
Where: output is a dbValue, input1 and input2 are dbValue defined by expressions.
This function returns 1 if the value of input1 is equal to input2.value. Otherwise, the returned value
is 0.
If both inputs are not constant values, the returned quality flag is input1.qFlag | input2.qFlag, and
the output timestamp is equal to the most recent of both inputs.
If only one of the inputs is not a constant value, the returned quality flag and timestamp are equal
to this input values for quality and timestamp.
If both inputs are constants, the returned quality and timestamp are equal to the second input
values for quality and timestamp.
Conditional operators have the lowest precedence between the operators.
This function is implemented according to the following pseudo-code:

If(input1.value == input2.value)
→ output.value = 1;
Else
→ output.value = 0;
If (input1.type != CONST)
→ output.qFlag = input1.qFlag && output.tSpec = input1.tSpec
If(input2.type != CONST) && input2.timestamp > input1.timestamp)
→ output.qFlag |= input2.qFlag
→ if(input2.tSpec > input1.tSpec)
→ output.tSpec = input2.tSpec
Else
→

output.qFlag = input2.qFlag && output.tSpec = input2.tSpec

If any of the input values are equal to NAN, the result value will be set as NAN (0 at status or
command) with invalid output quality flag.
Example
input1 == 3
Given that input1 is coreDb point name, this function will return 1 if input1.value is equal to 3. If not,
the returned value is equal to 0:
output.qFlag = input1.qFlag
output.tSpec = input1.tSpec

Pag 155

User Manual – Easergy Builder

06/2024

6.3.17

CoreDb Points
Formula Device also accepts a coreDb point name as a valid coordinate. In this case, the
calculation function consists of copy the value of this coreDb point and refresh it when is needed.
The Device firstly look for the coreDb point name at Status, secondly at Command, followed by
Analog and finally Setpoint type.
If the quality flag from the coreDb point name has the Not Topical or Invalid (local or remote), the
Invalid Local quality flag will be set to 1.
If its value is equal to NAN, the value copied to formula variable is NAN (0 at status or command)
with invalid quality flag and current local timestamp.

Variables
There are two special variables used in the Formula Device:
FORM_PERIOD
FORM_CYCLETIME
FORM_PERIOD
This variable name will get the value of the waiting time between all non-triggered expressions are
calculated on a cycle and the next one.
Its quality flag is equal to 0 (GOOD) and its timestamp is the date of the Formula Device
initialization.
FORM_CYCLETIME
This variable name gets the last duration of the Device entry calculation. Its unit is in milliseconds.
Its quality flag is equal to 0 (GOOD) and its timestamp is the local date of the end of the last
formula calculation cycle time.
If any of these variable names are set up as coreDb point names, the Formula Device will get them
as a normal coreDb point name variable.

Constant Values
Formula Device accepts a constant value (integer or float, positive or negative) as coordinate. In
this case, the coordinate numeric value will be copied as its value.

User Manual – Easergy Builder

Foreword
Safety Information
Important Information
Read these instructions carefully, and look at the equipment to become familiar
with the device before trying to install, operate, service, or maintain it. The
following special messages may appear throughout this documentation or on the
equipment to warn of potential hazards or to call attention to information that
clarifies or simplifies a procedure.
The addition of this symbol to a “Danger” or “Warning” safety label indicates that an
electrical hazard exists which will result in personal injury if the instructions are not
followed.

This is the safety alert symbol. It is used to alert you to potential personal injury
hazards. Obey all safety messages that follow this symbol to avoid possible injury or
death.

!

DANGER

DANGER indicates a hazardous situation which, if not avoided, will result in death or serious
injury.

!

WARNING

WARNING indicates a hazardous situation which, if not avoided, could result in death or
serious injury.

!

CAUTION

CAUTION indicates a hazardous situation which, if not avoided, could result in minor or
moderate injury.

NOTICE
NOTICE is used to address practices not related to physical injury.

Please Note
Electrical equipment should be installed, operated, serviced, and maintained only
by qualified personnel. No responsibility is assumed by Schneider Electric for any
consequences arising out of the use of this material.
A qualified person is one who has skills and knowledge related to the construction
and operation of electrical equipment and its installation, and has received safety
training to recognize and avoid the hazards involved.

About the Book
Document Scope
This manual provides information for the use of the webApp maintenance and
monitoring tool included in the Baseline Software Platform integrated in Saitel and
PowerLogicTM T500 devices of Schneider Electric.
To suit the different Saitel and T500 platforms, “RTU” terminology is used
throughout the document, as the name for the following modules:
•

PowerLogic HU280

•

SM_CPU866e

•

HUe

Screenshots included in this manual are related to PowerLogic HU280 module,
and are given as examples.

off.

•

To open a live current transformer secondary circuit, turn off the primary side
of the transformer and short-circuit the transformer secondary circuit.

•

Set all devices, doors, and covers before turning on power to this unit.

Failure to follow these instructions will result in death or serious injury.

webApp - Maintenance and Monitoring Tool

webApp First Contact

webApp First Contact
Baseline Software Platform
Introduction
The baseline software platform of Schneider Electric consists of the following
components:
•

Real-time operating system (RTOS): VxWorks, Linux, ...

•

Real-time applications and configuration files (XML format)

•

Configuration, management, supervision and monitorization tools
NOTE: The basic head units of the Saitel DR, HU_B and HU_BI modules do
not run VxWorks nor Linux. They operate with a tailored-made OS which
includes the database and applications.

The following figure shows the different applications included in the software
platform, as well as additional applications (devices) implementing devices or
protocols to upgrade Easergy Builder configuration tool:
Baseline software platform
IEC104
(client & server)

coreDb
Configuration Files

IEC103
(client)
IEC101
(client & server)
DNP
(client & server)
Modbus
(client & server)

Devices

(client & server)

Real Time Database
(coreDb)

Web Server
(webApp & webUI)

ISaGRAF

Synchronization
Supervision

Communications
Local Acquisition

Others
Devices

Real Time Operating System
(VxWorks & Linux)

The operating system abstracts the hardware from the software applications and
manages the applications in real-time. The operating system integrates the basic
protocols to access the remote unit and manage multiple users.
The real-time database, named coreDb, is the most important element. All the
other elements are developed around coreDb.

8

BQT7944802-01

webApp First Contact

webApp - Maintenance and Monitoring Tool

Device

Ed2 Device

IEDs

The following concepts are related to coreDb:
•

Device Controller (also referred to as controller): real-time application that
accesses coreDb. Each controller acts as a producer and/or consumer of
information managed by coreDb.

•

Point: each register of coreDb is a point. A point can be included in the table
Status, Analog, Command or Setpoint.

•

Device: a set of IO points that share a common source/destination. A typical
example of a device is an IED that communicates with the RTU, or the
representation of a SCADA exchanging information acquired or generated by
the RTU. A device is always associated to a type of controller.

•

Source: origin of the value of a coreDb data point. Any coreDb data point in
Command or SetPoint tables can have numerous different sources (in one or
several devices).
For Status and Analog tables, data point can only have one source.

•

Destination: target of the value of a coreDb data point. You can configure
coreDb data points to have several different destinations (in one or several
devices).

•

Coordinate: point identification within a device. It is unique for each point and
has a different structure for each controller. It is described in detail in the
appropriate manual of each controller.

•

Configuration Plugin: specific configuration plugins extend the RTU
configuration tool application to configure device controllers. Refer to Device
configuration plugins, page 10.
You can modify the configuration of each controller and device using the
appropriate plug-in. When the database is completely configured, the files with
the new information can be generated and transferred to the RTU. On startup,
the software processes the information in RTU.
NOTE: The configuration information exchanged between the RTU and
Easergy Builder is not continuous, but performed through XML files under
your request. When the configuration is modified in Easergy Builder and
the XML files are sent to the RTU, it is necessary to reboot the RTU.

coreDb – Real Time Database (RTDB)
The coreDb is the real-time database backend on which Baseline Software
Platform is built. All the information controlled and managed by the system is
stored in this database.

BQT7944802-01

9

webApp - Maintenance and Monitoring Tool

webApp First Contact

The functionalities of the system can be easily expanded to manage new
protocols, customized controllers, etc. To do this, trained developers need to
implement the required device controller and the associated configuration plugin
for Easergy Builder that allows you to configure the extended functionality.
The coreDb records are also called data points or, simply points (this term is used
onwards to avoid confusing these with device points).
The coreDb points are organized in four tables status, analog, setPoint and
command to group the different types of points. These internal tables present the
following differences:
•

Depending on the point type: The status and command tables support integer
values, whereas setpoint and analog tables manage floating values.

•

Depending on the treatment of the point: The status and analog points can
be locked or reset to initial values, whereas the other two signal types cannot
be locked or reset. All types retain value in non-volatile memory.

Devices
Each type of device keeps a list of its associated points, identified by unique
labels. These labels allow the identification of each device point unequivocally as
the source or destination of a coreDb data point.
Each point is a piece of information produced (or consumed) by a device. Within a
single device, the point identifiers (coordinates) are unique and cannot be used by
two different points.
PowerLogic T500 configuration tool supports the following device configuration
plugins:
•

DNP 3.0 client and server

•

Formula

•

IEC101 client and server

•

IEC104 client and server

•

IEC103 client

•

IEC61850 client Ed1

•

IEC61850 Ed2 client and server

•

ISAGRAF5

•

local acquisition

•

MODBUS client and server (with several profiles)

•

SNMP manager v1, v2c, and v3, SNMP agent v3

•

SOE (Sequence of Events management)

•

TSV (Terminal Server)

•

webUI

Software Tools
A basic configuration is included, and can be customized.

10

•

Easergy Builder: engineering tool for the RTU OFFLINE configuration. It
allows to include and adapt the different functions of the RTU to the system
where it is integrated.

•

CAE: engineering tool to define the security policy and assign roles to users. It
allows the definition of a series of rights and responsibilities in the system for
authorized users. It defines who, what, when and how to perform tasks
according to the Role-Based Access Configurations (RBAC) model. It is a
software tool that needs to be installed on a PC.

BQT7944802-01

webApp First Contact

webApp - Maintenance and Monitoring Tool

•

webApp: web tool for online maintenance and monitoring of the RTU. Using
the configuration defined in Easergy Builder and loaded in the RTU, you can
refer and/or change some parameters through the web server. Unlike Easergy
Builder, webApp does not allow adding new features. Only the parameters
included in the configuration can be changed.

•

ESM: EcoStruxure™ System Management tool that provides an Inventory
Baseline Management (IBM) software, capable to perform maintenance on
connected equipment, in an heterogeneous solution with a focus on
cybersecurity:

•

BQT7944802-01

◦

Data Management: storage and backup while tracking the evolution of
Firmware, Configuration, Setting Files and Documentation.

◦

Manages firmware and configuration upload.

◦

Monitors real-time changes firmware and configuration version for
connected devices.

◦

Cybersecurity: manages Role-Based Access Configurations for Schneider
Electric devices.

◦

Collects Syslogs from Schneider Electric connected devices.

webUI: web-based graphical substation user interface. This tool can be used
in two different environments:
◦

webUI (run-time): web tool running in the RTU, used to monitor and control
the information using screens previously designed by the user with the
configuration tool.

◦

webUI Add-on: edition tool included in Easergy Builder for creating and
editing customized webUI screens. It is used to design new screens for the
configuration loaded into the RTU or also modify the information displayed
in those already available.

Monitoring & Control
Use the Monitoring & Control menu to monitor the system information and
control data in coreDb.

System Information Page
Select Monitoring & Control > System Information:
System information (showing missing information)

All information shown in this window is supplied by the supervision device. Each
supervision point must be included in the configuration if you need to monitor the
data in real time. If a point is not included in the configuration, a notice is shown.
In the above example, you can see the following notices:
•

Configuration zone: PLC information not available. The point FAIL_PLC is
not included in the loaded configuration or an ISaGRAF device is not included.

•

CPU usage: N/A: the point CPU USAGE is not included in coreDb.

•

RAM usage: N/A: the point RAM USAGE is not included in coreDb.

In Easergy Builder,
1. create an ISaGRAF device,
2. select points FAIL_PLC, CPU_USAGE, and MEM_USAGE for supervision
device,
3. send this configuration to the RTU for the information to be available in
webApp.

Auto (default: 56 packets).

•

Interface: Select an interface manually from the available list.

•

IP Address: Enter the IP address of an interface.

BQT7944802-01

Working with webApp

webApp - Maintenance and Monitoring Tool

Working with webApp
Access to webApp
Introduction
The RTU module is provided with a standard security policy and a default RBAC
(Role-Based Access Control) model.
This model is defined and managed by a special tool - CAE (EcoStruxure
Cybersecurity Admin Expert - Security Administration Tool). Based on this model,
using webApp, authorized users can create and manage other users in the
system.

CAE and RBAC model
The CAE is the security administration tool for managing the security policy and
defining the restrictions for accessing the system or the communication interfaces.
The CAE is a software tool to be installed on a PC.
The RTU security policy consists in structuring the rights and responsibilities
within the system and defining who is authorized to do what, when, and how,
based on an RBAC (role-based access control) model.
A key aspect of the RBAC model is that any access is controlled by roles. A role is
essentially a set of permissions that you receive by the role to which you are
assigned, or by the roles that you inherit through the hierarchy of roles.
This RBAC model allows the RTU to:
•

Secure local and remote connections for maintenance: HTTPS, SSH (only
available for SM_CPU866e and HUe).

•

Secure file transfer protocols: SFTP (only available for SM_CPU866e and
HUe).

The RTU is supplied with a predefined RBAC model allowing different levels of
user access adapted to the CPU usage compliant with standard IEC 62351-8.
It is not necessary to modify the default RBAC model if it is suitable. The CAE is
not used for general use of the modules. The CAE can be used to create its own
user database along with definition of its own roles, as well as to manage the
RBAC models for each device centrally.

Default Users
The CAE tool can be used during the engineering phase to define or change the
system access restrictions, including the access rights and responsibilities (based
on an RBAC model).
Since the roles and access levels are already predefined in the RBAC model,
webApp is used to add or delete users, modify passwords, and assign or modify
one or more predefined roles to users.
In the default user database, the SecurityAdmin user is the only user with
sufficient rights (SECADM role) to manage cybersecurity actions. Only this user
can manage/modify user passwords and rights (roles).
You cannot modify the rights assigned to a role using webApp.

BQT7944802-01

Failure to follow these instructions can result in death, serious injury, or
equipment damage.

NOTE: For security purposes, default password is forced to be changed at
first login (only for PowerLogic T500 and HU280, from release 11.08.00
onwards).
Upon first login, the user is prompted to create a new password that meets the
following requirements:
•

Both upper case and lower case letters

•

At least one number

•

At least one special character

•

A minimum length of 12 characters

Since the default password is not aligned with previous requirements, it cannot be
reused.

Roles
RBAC (Role-based-access-control) is a control mechanism that defines roles
which have certain privileges. These privileges are defined by objects and the
action that you can do with them.
The following table shows actions allowed for each object type. Depending on
your role in which you logged in webApp, an action is or not allowed.

System Security Parameters
The Cybersecurity involves a certain number of system parameters that are
applied to the security policy. These parameters are summarized in the table
below and can be modified in the CAE tool.
Parameter

Description

Idle session timeout

After a period of inactivity, your access to the web server lapses. You must re-enter their username
and password to reconnect (Default: 15 minutes).

Authorize user lockout

The option is to lock/unlock a user account (Default: Authorized).

Maximum number of connections

The maximum number of connections attempts you can make (Default: 5 attempts).

Password timeout

The maximum time period allowed for entering password (Default: 3 minutes).

Account auto-unlock

Automatic unlocking of a user account after a defined duration (see below) if the account has been
locked out (Default: unlocking enabled).

Account lock duration

The maximum time period for an account to remain locked (Default: 240 seconds).

32

webApp - Maintenance and Monitoring Tool

Parameter

Description

Password complexity

The choice of policy (strength) used for creating or modifying passwords. Three levels of
complexity are possible that are compliant with standards:
•

•

•

Monitoring and recording standards

None (Default)
◦

One character minimum.

◦

Valid characters: ASCII [33,122]

IEEE 1686–2013:
◦

Eight characters minimum.

◦

Valid characters: ASCII [33,122], including:
–

1 lower case letter.

–

1 upper case letter.

–

1 digit.

–

1 special character
(! " # $ % & ' ( ) * , - . / : ; < = > ? @ [ ] ^_ `).

NERC:
◦

Six characters minimum.

◦

Valid characters: ASCII [33,122], including:
–

1 letter

–

1 digit

–

1 non-alphanumeric character

webUI
NOTE: webUI is available for PowerLogic HU280 module, Saitel DP
SM_CPU866e and EasyLogic T150 HUe.
In this Home tab there is a check box that makes it possible to enter webUI by
default instead of webApp.

The webUI is a web-based graphical user interface of the substation.
The webUI has two different environments:
•

webUI (real-time): it allows you to monitor graphically the information included
in the different screens designed by you through the editor included in Easergy
Builder (webUI Add-on).

•

webUI Editor: (webUI Add-on): the editor included in Easergy Builder for
creating and editing webUI screens. It allows you to design new screens for
the configuration loaded into the RTU or modify the information displayed in
those already available.

From webApp, you can go to webUI by selecting Monitoring & Control > webUI.
Then, it must introduce the user/password and the main webUI screen is shown.
To return to webApp, click on the settings button (

38

) and click on webApp.

WARNING
HAZARD OF EDITING LOCKED VALUES
Ensure that any locked and edited data values are valid to avoid an impact on
the control sequences of the system.
Failure to follow these instructions can result in death, serious injury or
equipment damage.

Click the
button associated with the Value field to manually edit the state or
value of a Command or Setpoint data item.
For a Status or Analog data item, you can force the status or value. This type of
data is only processed in read mode; forcing is only applied in simulation. To do
this, the actual data item must be locked first by clicking the Locking option.
Once the data is locked, the Edit button for this point is enabled and you can
change the status or value of the point in simulation mode.

42

BQT7944802-01

Working with webApp

webApp - Maintenance and Monitoring Tool

Disabling the Locking option cancels the simulation and returns to the actual
status or value of the data item.

Quality Information
Two icons displayed in the Quality column indicate the data processing quality.
The quality of a data item can indicate the validity of the status or value entered on
the web server page.
The quality is indicated for the following two sources:
•

Local source: reflects the quality of the data item from the viewpoint of its
processing at the CPU end.

•

Remote source: reflects the quality of the data item sent by the information
source (device) processing the data.

In the same way as for a change of state or value for Status or Analog data, you
can manually simulate the quality of a data item. To do this, first lock the data item
using the Locking option. Then click the
button associated with the Quality
field. The possible choices for the local and remote source quality are then
displayed on the screen.

BQT7944802-01

43

webApp - Maintenance and Monitoring Tool

Working with webApp

Disabling the Locking option cancels the simulation and returns to the actual
quality of the data item.
The table below shows the correspondence between the different quality types
that can be simulated after locking a data element. Note that this also gives an
indication of the different quality types that can be obtained in actual operation.
Quality information

44

Local Source Quality

Description

Overflow

An overflow has occurred on a counter.

Rollover

An overflow and an automatic reset have occurred on a counter.

Counter adjustment

The counter has been adjusted.

Chatter

Excessive change on a digital input.

Locked

The data item is locked.

Manual

The data item has been manually replaced.

Not typical

The data item has not yet been written to the database.

Invalid data

Data item is invalid.

Critical alarm

The value of the data item has exceeded the High-High alarm
threshold.

High level alarm

The value of the data item has exceeded the High alarm threshold.

Low level alarm

The value of the data item is below the Low alarm threshold.

Signal alarm

The value of the data item is below the Low-Low alarm threshold.

Invalid time

The data item time-stamp is invalid or inaccurate (the CPU is not
synchronized by a source)

Rollover

Chatter

The data item is locked.

Diagnostic and Maintenance
Introduction
NOTE: The features available in Diagnostic and Maintenance page depend
on the user’s role and rights.
The Diagnostic menu is used to view the data logs recorded in real time by the
RTU: events, system log, TCP traffic, or cybersecurity log (Security administration
rights are required).
The Maintenance menu helps with maintenance of the RTU: users management
(Security administration rights are required), system clock, IP configuration,
software upgrade and configuration management.

Diagnostic
Events Log Page
Select Diagnostic > Events to view the information recorded in SOE.
Events log

•

Sorted, by using the arrow next to the name of the column.

•

Filtered, by typing the text to search in the textbox under the name of the
column.

•

Cleared, by using

•

Exported to a csv file and downloaded to the PC.

button.

IMPORTANT: Be careful while clearing the events.
Take into account that clearing events do not only clear the table but also the
content of the SOE files in the file system. Events cannot be recovered after
confirming deletion.

IP Configuration
Select Maintenance > IP Configuration to access the IP Configuration page.

BQT7944802-01

53

webApp - Maintenance and Monitoring Tool

Working with webApp

Users with the correct privilege can change the configuration assigned to the
Ethernet ports, PRP, Bond, HSR, RSTP (not available for HU280) protocols and
routers.
IP Configuration - Devices

For each ETH port, you can set IP address, Subnet Mask, and Disable/Enable this
interface.
IP address can be set as Fixed or Automatic (using DHCP):

Redundant devices (PRP, HSR, BOND or RSTP for HU280, HUe and SM_
CPU866e) can only be configured when both of their single devices are not
configured.

54

BQT7944802-01

Working with webApp

webApp - Maintenance and Monitoring Tool

For instance, if LAN1 or LAN2 are configured with an IP address in HU280, then
PRP1, HSR1 and BOND1 are disabled:

NOTE: Multiple IPs can be configured for each interface, but these IPs must
not be on the same subnet. IPs within the same subnet cannot be configured
for different ports.
The router devices are used to define connection rules for accessing the RTU
remotely by other IP networks. It allows devices, such as tablets or PCs that are
connected to different auxiliary LANs than the RTU, to access the RTU via a WAN
within defined connection rules and limits.
IP Configuration - Routers

Refer to SE-S856-MSS - Easergy Builder User Manual for more information about
routers and ethernet ports configuration.
Routers can be added, edited or deleted depending on user’s role and rights.
Adding or editing a router

Where:

BQT7944802-01

•

Subnet IP: IP address of the auxiliary network needs access the RTU.

•

Subnet Mask: Used to define the usable IP address range on the auxiliary
network and limit the possible number of hosts.

•

Router IP: Address of the IP network gateway to which the RTU is connected.

55

webApp - Maintenance and Monitoring Tool

Working with webApp

Use 0.0.0.0 to define a default route through which communication is
automatically routed by the router when a connection is established. To do this,
simply configure the router parameters as follows:
•

Subnet IP: 0.0.0.0

•

Subnet Mask: 0.0.0.0

•

Router IP: IP address of the default gateway through which traffic is directed.

When all changes have been done, press button Save to store all modifications in
the RTU.
Depending on the configured device, the tab SNMP can be available for
configuring RTU as SNMP agent.
IP configuration - SNMP

A view is a user-defined label (collection of) subset of the MIB (Management
Information Base) tree. Each subset ID is defined by the OID (Object Identifier) of
the root of the relevant subtrees.
For each selected item in the Views list, the RTU creates a view with the selected
items. Each item in the list represents an OID (Object Identifier).
So with this section user can select what information (OID) the RTU will make
available for the SNMP manager connected to the RTU.

Click Edit SNMP user to change username, encryption algorithm or password.

webApp - Maintenance and Monitoring Tool

Firmware and Configuration Management
In the case the system performing in an invalid state, a software watchdog resets
the device to return it to a known state. The device reset is recorded in both the
Syslog and Cyber Security log.
The watchdog can reset the device up to 5 times, then the reset is disabled.
In the case the reset is disabled, do one of the following actions:
•

If there is access to webApp: search for the debug info through webApp to
report the issue and to apply another configuration.

•

If there is no access to webApp: perform a factory reset. Once set to factory
default mode, access to webApp and follow instructions just above.

The PowerLogic HU280 does not allow invalid configurations. PowerLogic HU280
manages configurations in the following way: PowerLogic HU280 always have a
backup of the last firmware image that was loaded successfully. When the
firmware is updated, the boot loader tries to launch the new firmware.
•

If the firmware load is successful: the firmware new version overwrites the
stored backup firmware for later use.

•

If the firmware load is not successful: the backed-up firmware version is
restored.

This protects the booting process in case of the installation of an improper
firmware.

Configuration Management
The initial configuration of the RTU should be completed in Easergy Builder to
carry out the following operations:
•

Configure protocols and devices

•

Configure device synchronization

•

Configure the SOE, etc.

Refer to SE-S856-MSS - Easergy Builder User Manual for more information about
configuration of the RTU.

BQT7944802-01

•

Copy a configuration stored in a device (slot) to the PC.

•

Load other configuration (the active configuration changes).

•

Copy a configuration in the PC to a device (slot) in the CPU.

SCADA and Client Protocols
The SCADA protocols are protocols that can be used for remote communication
between the RTU and the SCADA system using any available communication
channels. These protocols are server type since the SCADA system is the client
for communication management and the RTU is the server.
Current protocols supported in webApp are: DNP3 server, IEC101 server, and
IEC104 server.
Client protocols are those protocols allowing the RTU to communicate as the
client with auxiliary equipment or an external IED installed in substations (server).
Current protocol supported in webApp is Modbus client.
Parameters displayed in webApp correspond to the application parameters of the
protocol installed in the RTU. These parameters can be customized according to
the use and the protocol parameter setting at the SCADA system end or IED.

BQT7944802-01

69

webApp - Maintenance and Monitoring Tool

Working with webApp

Configuring SCADA protocols in RTU

For each communication protocol to be configured, refer to the communication
protocol user manual for more information.

SNMP Manager settings configuration
NOTE: This function is only available for PowerLogic T500 (HU280), from
release 11.08.00 onwards.
To access the settings of all SNMP clients configured, select Settings >
PowerLogic HU280 > Client protocols > SNMP Manager.
SNMP Manager configuration

70

BQT7944802-01

Working with webApp

webApp - Maintenance and Monitoring Tool

The different configured SNMP clients are shown as tabs below, along with the
periods. The configurable fields in the client are:
•

Version: 1, 2C or 3

•

Retries: Number of retry attempts if no response is received

•

Timeout: Time (in milliseconds) to wait for a response before considering the
request failed

•

IP: IP address of the SNMP server

•

Port: Communication port for SNMP

•

Authentication protocol: only for v3, can be MD5, SHA1 or SHA224

•

Username: Authentication username

•

Password: Authentication password (only for version 3)

The configurable fields in the period are:
•

Name of the period

•

Period in ms

Two synchronization sources are defined: the primary device and secondary
device. The secondary device is used if the primary device is unavailable.
Both synchronization devices can use any of the available synchronization
sources. The selection and the associated configuration are made using Easergy
Builder. Refer to SE-S856-MSS - Easergy Builder User Manual for more
information.

configuration loaded.

webApp - Maintenance and Monitoring Tool

Glossary
A
AX160: PowerLogic T500 analog input/output module.

B
BDEW: German Association of Energy and Water Industries.

C
CAE: Cybersecurity Administrator Expert. The CAE is a tool for organizing the
T500 security policy and defining system access restrictions and user rights.
CoreDB: CoreDB is a real-time RTU database that helps ensure the RTU signals
are managed in real-time.
CPU: Central Processing Unit.
CS Phase I: Cybersecurity Phase I.

D
DHCP: Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol. A network protocol for ensuring that
station IP parameters are automatically configured via automatic IP address
allocation.

E
E3: Electrical Energy Efficiency.
Easergy Builder: Easergy Builder is the official configuration tool for T500
equipment. This tool can be used to configure all the advanced RTU functions (for
example, database, protocol addressing, events) and to carry out maintenance
tasks on the equipment.

G
GPS: Global Positioning System.

H
HSR: High-availability Seamless Redundancy.
HTTP: Hyper Text Transfer Protocol. Client-server communication protocol
developed for the World Wide Web. HTTPS (where S = Secure) is the secure
variant of HTTP using SSL or TLS protocols.
HU280: PowerLogic T500 CPU module.

I
IEC: International Electrotechnical Commission. The international standards
organization for the fields of electricity, electronics, electromagnetic compatibility,
nanotechnology, and related technologies. It complements the International
Organization for Standardization (ISO), which is responsible for other fields.
IED: Intelligent Electronic Device.
IEEE: Institute of Electrical and Electronics Engineers. The IEEE is a professional
association comprising members from the fields of electrical engineering,
information technology, telecommunications, etc. The organization's mission is to
advance knowledge in the field of electrical engineering and publish associated
standards and other texts written by its members.
BQT7944802-01

75

webApp - Maintenance and Monitoring Tool

IP: Internet Protocol.
IRIG-B: mode B of the standard IRIG.

L
LAN: Local Area Network.
Linux: Operating system running in realtime on the software platform. This is the
element in charge of extracting the various software components from the
hardware.

N
NERC CIP: North American Electric Reliability Corporation Critical Infrastructure
Protection plan.

O
OS: Operating System.

P
PC: Personal Computer.
PLC: Programmable Logic Controller.
PRP: Parallel Redundancy Protocol.
PTP: Precision Time Protocol.

R
RSTP: Rapid Spanning Tree Protocol.
RTDB: Real-Time DataBase.
RTOS: Real-Time Operating System.
RTU: Remote Terminal Unit.

S
SCADA: Supervisory Control And Data Acquisition.
SNMP: Simple Network Management Protocol.
SNTP: Simple Network Time Protocol.
SOE: Sequence Of Events. This relates to saving and storing events and signal
changes in the RTU that manages the data in real-time.
SSH: Secure SHell. SSH is both a program and a secure communication protocol.
It is the equivalent of the Telnet program and is used in the same way to run
certain operations remotely (mainly server administration or file transfer
operations). SSH is more secure than Telnet protocol.

T
TCP or TCP/IP: Transmission Control Protocol/Internet Protocol. Set of protocols
used to transfer data on the Internet.
TLS: Transport Security Layer.

76

BQT7944802-01

webApp - Maintenance and Monitoring Tool

W
WAN: Wide Area Network.
webUI: Advanced tool for the design and operation of a local web-based
substation user interface.

X
XS280: PowerLogic T500 serial communication module.

Information on Non-Inclusive or Insensitive Terminology
As a responsible, inclusive company, Schneider Electric is constantly updating its
communications and products that contain non-inclusive or insensitive
terminology. However, despite these efforts, our content may still contain terms
that are deemed inappropriate by some customers.

Safety Instructions
DANGER

HAZARD OF ELECTRIC SHOCK, EXPLOSION, OR ARC FLASH
Apply appropriate personal protective equipment (PPE) and follow safe
electrical work practices. See NFPA 70E, NOM-029-STPS-2011, CSA Z462,
NFC 18–510 and UTE C18–510–1.
This unit must be installed and serviced only by qualified electrical
personnel.
Turn off all power supplies of this unit before working on or inside the unit.
Always use a properly rated voltage sensing device to confirm that power is
off.
To open a live current transformer secondary circuit, turn off the primary side
of the transformer and short-circuit the transformer secondary circuit.
Set all devices, doors, and covers before turning on power to this unit.
Failure to follow these instructions will result in death or serious injury.

BQT7944803-EN-00

3

webUI – Add-on & Runtime

webUI Runtime

webUI Runtime
webApp & webUI
Saitel DP, DR and PowerLogic T500 RTUs support two web-based tools for
maintenance and monitoring:

• webApp: Embedded webserver interface for monitoring, maintenance &

diagnostics of the RTU. Using the configuration defined in Easergy Builder and
loaded on the CPU, the user can consult and / or change some parameters
through webApp. Unlike Easergy Builder, webApp does NOT allow to add new
features (and / or screens). It can only change the information previously
included through Easergy Builder using the screens already defined in the tool.

• webUI: Web-based graphical substation user interface. This tool is composed
by two different environments, one for design-time and another for run-time:

o webUI (Runtime): Web tool running in the RTU allowing to monitor the
information using screens which were designed by the user with Easergy
Builder (webUI Add- On).
o webUI (Add-On): Additional tool included in Easergy Builder for creation
and addition of customized webUI screens. It allows to design new screens
for the configuration loaded into the RTU or to modify the information in
those screens already loaded into the RTU.

Accessing webUI
webUI is accessible through a compatible web browser running in an OS Windows 7®
or higher.
The most common browsers (Firefox and Chrome) support this tool, although only
versions supporting HTML5.
Web browser

Minimal Version

Google
Chrome

58

Firefox

52

Microsoft
Edge

Microsoft Edge
44.18362.449.0 Microsoft
Edge HTML 18.18363

Internet
Explorer

Not compatible

Using webApp & webUI
There are two web-based tools available: webApp and webUI.
By default, webApp is used as main web tool, that is, when the user writes
https://<CPU IP address> in the address bar of the web browser, by default, the main
page of webApp is shown.

webUI Runtime

webApp in URL

The user can change the default landing page to webUI activating the following
checkbox on the main page of webApp:
webUI as main app option

Next time, webUI will be used as main web tool
If the browser displays a security message when accessing the RTU, it can be
dismissed to load the website. For example, in Microsoft Edge select 'Go on to the
webpage'.

NOTICE

HAZARD OF INAPROPRIATE OPERATION OR INFORMATION EXPOSURE
It is recommended to change the default passwords, and in any case, follow the
security policy applicable in each control system.
Failure to following this recommendation may compromise the operation of
the system and its information
In the case of PowerLogic T500, by default, there are no users with access to
webUI. Security Admin user can create a user with a role with webUI access.
See the BQT7944802 webApp User Manual for more information about the users
defined in the system and the roles assigned to each of them.
From webApp, the user can access to webUI selecting Monitoring & control ->
webUI in the main menu. A login window will be shown, and the user needs to have
permission to use the webUI to access the interface.
To return to webApp from webUI, select webApp in the menu tab available when
'Settings' button is clicked.

Main Zones
Once inside the webUI tool, the user can see three main zones:
Description of the webUI environment

• 1: Session Information.
• 2: Graphic representation zone.
• 3: Alarms zone.

Session Information (Zone 1)
This section is always visible, and it indicates the connection status to the webUI
server. If the connection is active, the information displayed will be refreshed in realtime from the server database.
Session information with CPU connection
If the connection to the webUI server is lost, this area will be shown in red, and the
latest valid information retrieved from the server will be illustrated.
Session information when the connection to the CPU is lost

• Customer logo:

Graphic Representation Zone (Zone 2)
The information displayed in the graphic representation area depends on the option
selected in the main menu.
Graphic representation zone

The first screen in the main menu is shown by default. The order of the available
screens can be changed using Easergy Builder.
In this zone it is possible to zoom and pan. Zooming is done with the mouse wheel
and panning can be done with the left-click, right-click or also with the mouse wheel.
When the window is in its original size, no panning is allowed.
Finally, to reset the view it would only be necessary to double left-click to do it.

Latest Alarms Zone (Zone 3)
This zone shows the latest three alarms. The visual appearance of each alarm line
will depend on its status and on the user acknowledge.
• Blinking: Unacknowledged alarm
• Colored according to the user configuration: Active alarm and Inactive alarm
colors are configured by the user in the Easergy Builder Add-On. More
information in section Alarm Levels.

Main menu (screens detail)

Using Easergy Builder 1.6.7.0 or later for Saitel RTUs and 1.7.32.0 for PowerLogic
T500 RTUs, the user can design new screens according project requirements. All
information is included in chapter webUI Editor of this manual.

• SLD or electrical diagrams.

SM_SER/XS280/AB_SER, communication ports, voltage, etc.

Public

webUI – Add-on & Runtime

webUI Runtime

Example of acquisition screen

When webUI is started, the first screen in the list appears by default. This list can be
configured in the graphic engineering tool webUI Add-On.

Alarms

More information in chapter webUI Editor.

Selecting button

> Alarms, the alarm page is shown. In this list are included:

• Acknowledged active alarms.
• Unacknowledged active alarms.
• Unacknowledged inactive alarms
Acknowledging an alarm will make it stop blinking. To acknowledge a specific
alarm, it is necessary to use the button “Acknowledge” in that row. There are two
more buttons: “Acknowledge all” and “Acknowledge page”. The first is used to
knowledge all activated alarms, while the second is used to acknowledge only the
page where the user is at that moment.
However, there is a particular case where it is necessary to use the “Acknowledge
page” or the specific “Acknowledge” and that is when the alarm corresponds to a
supervision point. “Acknowledge all” will not acknowledge alarms which correspond
to supervision points.
Alarms page

The color of the alarm is configurable in Easergy Builder. More information in
paragraph Alarm Levels.
Alarms are, by default, ordered by the column 'Timestamp'. The arrow next to it can
be used to show most recent or oldest first.
For each signal, the displayed information is platform dependent:

BQT7944803-EN-00

Public

11

webUI – Add-on & Runtime

webUI Runtime

Saitel DP and Saitel DR
There are two different views:
• Standard: Available columns are TIMESTAMP, VALUE, QUALITY, POINT
NAME, POSITION, SOURCE and DESCRIPTION
Alarms page (Saitel standard view)

• Engineering: Available columns are TIMESTAMP, VALUE, QUALITY, B1, B2,
B3, ELEMENT and INFO
Alarms page (Saitel engineering view)

PowerLogic T500
There are four different views:
• Standard: Available columns are ALARM, TIMESTAMP, VALUE, QUALITY,
POINT NAME, POSITION, SOURCE, DESCRIPTION and ALARM TYPE
Alarms page (PowerLogic T500 standard view)

• Integrated view: Available columns are TIMESTAMP, VALUE, QUALITY,
POINT NAME, POSITION, SOURCE and DESCRIPTION
Alarms page (PowerLogic T500 integrated view)

• Engineering: Available columns are ALARM, TIMESTAMP, VALUE,
QUALITY, B1, B2, B3, ELEMENT, INFO and ALARM TYPE

12

Public

BQT7944802-00

webUI – Add-on & Runtime

webUI Runtime

Alarms page (PowerLogic T500 engineering view)

• Integrated view + Engineering: Available columns are TIMESTAMP, VALUE,
QUALITY, B1, B2, B3, ELEMENT and INFO
Alarms page (PowerLogic T500 integrated + engineering view)

The meanings of these fields are:
• ALARM: Defines if the signal es in alarm status with a blinking red “A”. This
column name is not visible.
• TIMESTAMP: Date and time associated to the alarm.
• VALUE: Value of the signal. For digital signals, the value is shown in text
format, and for analog signals the numerical value is used.
• QUALITY: Signal quality.
• B1: The text indicated in this column will be applied to the engineering field B1
assigned to the point in the webUI configuration.
• B2: The text indicated in this column will be applied to the engineering field B2
assigned to the point in the webUI configuration.
• B3: The text indicated in this column will be applied to the engineering field B3
assigned to the point in the webUI configuration.
• ELEMENT: The text indicated in this column will be applied to the Engineering
Element field assigned to the point in the webUI configuration.
• POINT NAME: Signal name in coreDb.
• POSITION: Source device of the information associated with the signal (field
Source Device).
• SOURCE: Coordinates of the signal in coreDb (field Source Coordinates).
• DESCRIPTION: Brief description of the signal (field Description in coreDb).
• ALARM TYPE: Indicates the “Alarm Mode” defined in Easergy Builders real
time table.
The information shown in the previous fields is configured for each signal in the
page 'Realtime configuration' of Easergy Builder webUI Add-Ons as follow:
• VALUE: Text included in field A0 label, A1 label, A2 label or A3 label
according to the signal value (0, 1, 2 or 3).
• B1, B2 and B3: Texts include in columns B1, B2 and B3 respectively.
• ELEMENT: Text included in the column Element.
• INFO: Text included in the column Info.
Clicking on any of these elements allows the user to order the alarms depending on
the value of that element.

BQT7944803-EN-00

Public

13

webUI – Add-on & Runtime

webUI Runtime

It is also possible to re-dimension the webUI tables columns in width.
Using the check box 'Not acknowledged', only unacknowledged alarms are included
in the list.

Signals

Alarm information can be downloaded to a file called 'Alarms.csv' which can be
imported into a commercial tool, for example Microsoft Excel®.

Select button
> Signals to access the information of signals in coreDb. The order
of the list is the same as in coreDb.
List of signals in database

This list includes only the signals that have been marked as Enabled in the
Realtime Configuration screen in Easergy Builder. More information in paragraph
Realtime Configuration
For each signal the following information is displayed:
• TIMESTAMP: Date and time of the last change of value or quality.
• VALUE: For status and command points, label assigned in Easergy Builder to
the current value of the signal. This label is set in the Realtime Configuration
window in Easergy Builder. If no label has been assigned, the test NA (not
apply) is shown. For analog and setpoint, this field shows the current value of
the signal. More information in section Realtime Configuration.
• QUALITY: Quality information.
• POINT NAME: Signal name in coreDb.
• POSITION: Source device of the information associated with the signal (field
Source Device).
• SOURCE: Coordinates of the signal in coreDb (field Source Coordinates).
• DESCRIPTION: Brief description of the signal (field Description in coreDb).
Activating 'Engineering' check box shown the engineering information associated to
each signal, as it can be seen in the following figure.
It is also possible to re-dimension the webUI tables columns in width.

14

Public

BQT7944802-00

webUI – Add-on & Runtime

webUI Runtime

List of signals in database with engineering information

Engineering values in this picture have been created only for this user manual
purpose. These values must be configured according to its engineering purpose in
the substation.
• B1: The text indicated in this column will be applied to the engineering field B1
assigned to the point in the webUI configuration.
• B2: The text indicated in this column will be applied to the engineering field B2
assigned to the point in the webUI configuration.
• B3: The text indicated in this column will be applied to the engineering field B3
assigned to the point in the webUI configuration.
• ELEMENT: The text indicated in this column will be applied to the Engineering
Element field assigned to the point in the webUI configuration.
• INFO: The text indicated in this column will be used to explain what the
corresponding signal does.
Clicking on any of these elements allows the user to order the signals depending on
the value of that element.
This information can be also enabled and configured in the Realtime Configuration
window. More information in Realtime Configuration paragraph.

Chronological
Select button

> Chronological to access the chronological information as follows:

Chronological events

The Chronological window shows all events that occur in the system, ordered by
the timestamp, being the first record in the list the most recent.
The number of events in the list will depend on the depth marked in the SOE
configuration in Easergy Builder. More information in the SOE Device user manual.
For each signal the following information is displayed:
• TIMESTAMP: Date and time of last value or quality change.

BQT7944803-EN-00

Public

15

webUI – Add-on & Runtime

webUI Runtime

• VALUE: For status points, this field shows the label assigned in Easergy
Builder to the value of the signal. This label is set in the Realtime
Configuration window in Easergy Builder. If no label has been assigned, the
test NA (not apply) is shown. For analog and setpoint, this field shows the
current value of the signal. More information in section 2.3.
• QUALITY: Signal quality.
• BX: The text indicated in this column will be applied to the engineering field Bx
assigned to the point in the webUI configuration. This field is only for labeling
purpose. The user defines how they are used to identify the element. For
example: B1 Substation name, B2 Zone and B3 Position.
• ELEMENT: Name of the element on the control window.
• INFO: Description of the element on the control window.
There is an option to download the chronological record of events by clicking on the
blue download button.
Clicking on any of these elements allows the user to order the signals depending on
the value of that element.
It is also possible to select a row (which will turn blue if it is selected) as well as redimensioning the Chronological tables columns in width.
Select row in Chronological tab

Dynamic Elements
Any screen can include two different types of symbols associated to field devices:
• Informative. This type of symbol is oriented to show signal values, status
information or any other data available in database.
• Controllable Elements. The associated device in field allows to be operated.
When a symbol on the screen is selected (left clicking with the mouse), a pop-up
window appears including information about the associated signals and operation
buttons if the device allows to be operated.

Informative Symbols
If an element does not allow operation, when right-clicking on it, a window similar to
the following one is shown:
Window of informative symbols

Where:
• Position: name of the equipment where the signal comes from.
• Name: name of the point.
• Description: descriptive text of the signal.
• Value: numerical value of the signal.
• Quality: quality information based on icons. More information below.

16

Public

BQT7944802-00

webUI – Add-on & Runtime

webUI Runtime

• Hour: timestamp of last value change.
• Status: state of the signal.
Click button EXIT to close the window.

Controllable Symbols
To understand how to use controllable symbols it is necessary to know first how
Local and Remote modes work.
First, the system mode can be modified as explained in section Settings Tool.
The difference between Local and Remote modes is the execution of commands.
For Local it is possible to execute them while in Remote mode it is not.
More information about activating commands and controllable symbols can be
found below.
Controllable Symbols can only be operated while webUI is in Local mode and the
current user has the suitable rights to execute the action (for instance, Viewer role
do not have permission to execute controllable symbols).
All commands are SBO (Select Before Operate), that is, commands are executed in
two phases, selection and execution.
When the user right clicks on a controllable element, a window will be shown
including operation buttons (according to the active configuration) and real-time
information in coreDb.
Window of controllable symbols

The operation button will be shown when CONTROL button is pressed. While the
command is not selected, the text 'Not selected' is shown in red.

BQT7944803-EN-00

Public

17

webUI – Add-on & Runtime

webUI Runtime

Initiating a command execution

The user needs to select CLOSED or OPEN and then to press SELECT. The status
will change to Selected:

18

Public

BQT7944802-00

webUI – Add-on & Runtime

webUI Runtime

Executing a command

To send the command to field, button EXECUTE must be pressed.
When in Remote mode, trying to execute a command will make a message appear,
saying “REMOTE commands not allowed”, as it can be seen in the following figure.
Executing a command in Remote mode

The Execute command is not allowed until the command is selected. To execute a
command user must press buttons in the following order: CONTROL, SELECT and
EXECUTE.
Finally, using 'Command lock' or 'Interlock' function, a command can be blocked
depending on the value of other elements in coreDb. More information in section
Command Locks.

Quality Information
The icon displayed next to each value, provides information about the quality of the
status or value shown. This information is shown too in the Signal list for each
signal.
The quality icon information is associated to a hexadecimal value, and it is
calculated based on a combination of each quality bit. Other data to be considered
is the status Local or Remote.
The table below shows the possible quality values associated to a signal:

Graphic Elements
Graphical windows of webUI are designed in Easergy Builder using graphical
object. Each graphical object has associated its properties and a predefined
behavior. Following paragraphs offer detailed information about the type of
elements that can be found in webUI.
In runtime, the color of the symbol is associated to the voltage level of the line
which it is included (main, second of third). The colors of these voltage lines are
configured in Easergy Builder for each screen by using the property windows. More
information can be found in chapter Graphic Objects (Zone 3) of this manual.

Measures
Several types of predefined measurements are available to be included in the
webUI screens. Some properties of these values, such as font, size or associated
text, can be changed using Easergy Builder as it is explained in the following
chapter of this manual.
The color associated to the value can be configured by the user.
Each measurement needs to be linked to a coreDb signal and its value will be
shown in real-time in webUI. Parameters like color, letter font, size, etc. will be
configurable. The “Units” parameter, which is used to describe the unit of
measurement, will be only available for “Measures” and not for “Analog value”.

webUI allows to design an acquisition screen including information of the devices.
The following table includes the device types available, and the information shown
for each one. The information shown for each module must be correctly linked to
the status signals in coreDb. More information can be found in section Status Point
of this manual.
In the following pictures green color means OK, while red color indicates a problem
has been detected in the element.

Settings Tool
Using the setting tool

, it is possible to:

• Restart the CPU or change the status between local and remote.
• Go to webApp.
• Manage interlocks.
• Activate / Deactivate sound for alarms.
• Activate / Deactivate pop-up windows of the dynamic elements on the
screens.
• Change the default language of webUI.
• Have information about the current webUI.

BQT7944803-EN-00

Public

29

webUI – Add-on & Runtime

webUI Runtime

Settings tool

Using System option, the following window is shown:
System operations

Press RESET to reboot the CPU or CHANGE L/R STATE to change between local
and remote control of the RTU.
After the button is pressed, the text Correct (green) or Error (red) is shown
depending on the operation was successfully executed or not.
To be able to use these buttons, the following signals must be defined in coreDb:
• For Local/Remote button: Table: Status, Source: supervision device,
Coordinate: LOCAL:W.
• However, there is something specific about this signal. If it is also mapped
with Destination: supervision device and Coordinate: LOCAL, if the signal
value is valid, then webUI does not allow to use the button to alternate
between Local and Remote modes. If it has an invalid value, it will appear as
“Unknown” (
) and webUI does not allow either to use the button.
• For Reset button: Table: Command, Coordinate: RESET_RTU.
Both signals must be configured as Enable in Realtime Configuration window in
Easergy Builder. More information in following chapter.
NOTICE
HAZARD OF INAPROPRIATE OPERATION
Before pressing RESET button, check that there is not a valid operation on
course: There is no confirmation required and the RTU will be rebooted
immediately.
Failure to follow these instructions can result in an improper operation.

Interlocks
To access the list of interlocks defined in webUI, select button

> Interlocks.

Interlocks in webUI
More information about how interlocks can be modified or created can be found in
Command Locks section.

BQT7944803-EN-00

31

webUI – Add-on & Runtime

webUI Editor

webUI Editor
webUI Description
The webUI Editor is designed as an Add-On included in the Easergy Builder
configuration tool.
In the configuration environment of Easergy Builder, on the main menu, select AddOns > webUI Configuration. If this option is not available, install the plugin
'webUI_SPlugin.msi' supplied in the Easergy Builder installation package.
webUI configuration tool completes the functionality of Easergy Builder with the
necessary functions to perform the configuration of the embedded webUI system.
This tool offers a series of windows to allow the monitoring and control of the
system.
With these considerations, webUI Configuration for Easergy Builder allows to
configure the screens included in the system, as well as the graphical objects that
will be represented in each of them.
webUI Editor

The webUI Editor is shown as one more tab in Easergy Builder (marked in blue in
the figure above).
This window has five different areas:
• Zone 1: a toolbar that includes a series of buttons with the following features:
•

Show or hide the different areas of the editor.

•

Show and hide the design grid.

•

Manage and use the graphical display repository.

•

Access to the editing help tools (object alignment).

•

Preview the selected screen.

• Zone 2: name and description of the screens created in the system, presented
in a hierarchical way.
• Zone 3: tree of graphic objects available to be used on the screens.
• Zone 4: editor area, where the user can design the aspect that will show in
real-time the screen selected in zone 2.
• Zone 5: configuration area, where screens, database and settings can be
configured.

32

Public

BQT7944802-00

webUI – Add-on & Runtime

webUI Editor

Screen Configuration
webUI Configuration (Zone 1)
webUI Editor allows the addition of new screens, which will be available in the web
interface. Right after creation the screens will be empty, thus allowing the user to
design them in accordance with project needs and based on the library of the
different available engineering elements (Zone 3).
The following buttons are available:
• Show/Hide screen list (Zone 2) >
• Show/Hide the list of available items (Zone 3) >
• Show/Hide the drawing grid (Zone 4) >
• System identification >
• Add new screens >
• Delete the screen selected in the list >
• Importing a screen from the repository. See Screen Organization >
• Include the selected screen in the repository. See Screen Organization >
• Access the repository for administration >
• Aligns the left side of the selected figures >
• Aligns the upper side of the selected figures >
• Aligns the right side of the selected figures >
• Aligns the bottom side of the selected figures >
• Aligns the selected figures vertically >
• It lines up the selected figures horizontally >
• It joins the selected figures horizontally >
• Shows the screen and its schema >

Create a Screen
To create a new screen, press the button
each parameter of the new screen.

or right click on zone 4 and configure

New screen configuration window

Where:
• 1st voltage color: Color that will be applied to the main voltage elements.
• 2nd voltage color: Color that will be applied to the second voltage elements.
• 3rd voltage color: Color that will be applied to the third voltage elements.
• 4th voltage color: Color that will be applied to the fourth voltage elements.
• 5th voltage color: Color that will be applied to the fifth voltage elements.
• 6th voltage color: Color that will be applied to the sixth voltage elements.
• 7th voltage color: Color that will be applied to the seventh voltage elements.
• Background color: Color to be applied to the screen background.
• Description: Description of the new screen.
• Name: Screen name.
• Screen schema: This schema determines the final image of the screen to the
user, determining the visual environment in which the screen will be
presented in the webUI.
• Size: It determines the total size of the screen, indicating the width and height
in pixels.
Changing any of the voltage levels will cause the switchgear objects included in the
screen to update their color to reflect the new configured voltages.
Press confirmation button and the new screen will be included in the list.

Hiding a Screen
To hide a screen from the runtime, select it, right click, and choose “Hide screen”. In
this case, the symbol
which appears on the left will turn its color to red
.
This means when the configuration is uploaded to the RTU, this screen will not
appear as an option.
If a screen is hidden, to make it visible again it would only be necessary to select it,
right click and choose “Make screen visible”. Then the symbol will turn back from
red to green.

To remove a screen from the list, select it and press the button
be displayed to confirm the operation or not:

Remove screen confirmation window

Screen Organization
The user can control the order in which the screens are displayed in real-time. The
order in webUI coincides with the order in which they appear in Easergy Builder.
webUI signal order

If the order is changed in Easergy Builder, the order in webUI changes too.

Screen Repository
This repository stores a set of screens that can be reused in other configurations.
New screens can be added to this repository for future use.
Screens are grouped according to the system requirements. These groups are
created and managed by the user.
To access the screen repository, press the button

.

Screen Repository

To include a screen in the repository, it is mandatory to select a group of screens.
To create a group, use the following button in the screen repository window:
If at least one group exists, a screen can be selected and store it using button
Selecting screen group for the repository

, a screen can be included in the current project.

Graphic Objects (Zone 3)
The main webUI configuration zone contains a tree of graphic objects where the
objects included in the loaded library are presented. Using these objects, new
graphic elements can be added to the active screen.
To do this, select an object type on the tree (it will be highlighted in blue), drag and
drop on the screen. When the object is located on the screen, clicking right-button,
its properties can be changed according to the requirements of the project.
Following sections show the available properties for dynamic and static objects. The
most of them are common for all object types and only some object types could
have properties which are explained below too.

command (point/signal that will allow to act on
the state of the object) and the signal
(point/signal that will provide the state of the
object) associated to it. This command value
can be modified through this object.

Text window. Some features can be selected, as
the color, letter font, etc.

Dynamic text according to the value of a signal.

It will be highlighted when the signal value is 0
and gray when it is 1.

Several types of predefined measurements are available to be included in the
webUI screens. Some properties of these values, such as font, size, or associated
text, can be changed using the properties windows.
Measure properties window

Analog value properties window

Each measurement needs to be linked to a coreDb signal and its value will be
shown in real-time in webUI. Parameters like color, letter font, size, etc. will be
configurable. The “Units” parameter, which is used to describe the unit of
measurement, will be only available for “Measures” and not for “Analog value”.
